english: Korean Beef Bone Broth Rice Soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Gomtang Gukbap; closest parent 'beef soup food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Gomtang Gukbap. 'Beef soup' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Gomtang Gukbap or a sufficiently close Korean beef soup with rice. SNOMED CT anchor omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (rice cake / tteok may contain wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Beef Gomtang Gukbap is approximately 600 g, comprising a large bowl of milky beef bone broth with tender beef shank pieces, a portion of steamed white rice (approximately 150–180 g cooked), sliced tteok, radish, and a soft-boiled or poached egg.

nutrient_source.title: Gomtang (beef bone soup) and Gukbap (rice in soup) — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef shank braised (NDB 13360), Cooked white rice long-grain (NDB 20045), Beef broth (NDB 06159), Egg whole cooked (NDB 01129), Sesame oil (NDB 04058), and Korean radish (approximated from daikon radish NDB 11429); weighted by estimated serving composition.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Beef Gomtang Gukbap as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each major ingredient (beef shank, white rice, beef broth, egg, sesame oil, radish). Estimated composition per 100 g of the combined dish (broth + solids + rice): approximately 55% broth, 25% cooked rice, 12% beef shank, 5% egg, 2% radish and tteok, 1% sesame oil. Values are cross-checked against published Korean food composition data from the Rural Development Administration (RDA) National Institute of Agricultural Sciences, Korea Food Composition Table 9.2 (2019), which reports Gomtang at approximately 28–35 kcal/100 g for the broth-only form; the full Gukbap dish with rice is substantially higher in energy and carbohydrate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled, Asian-style) GI approximately 64–72; beef broth and protein components GI effectively 0; composite dish GI estimated at 54 based on carbohydrate fraction from rice dominating the glycemic response, weighted by available carbohydrate per serving.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Gomtang Gukbap. The glycemic response of this dish is dominated by the cooked white rice component. Using the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, medium-high) and applying a dilution factor for the broth, beef, and egg components (which contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate), the composite dish GI is estimated at approximately 54. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 or less is classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as: (11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 600 g / 100) × 54 / 100 = 69 g available carbohydrate × 0.54 = approximately 37.3 g GL, rounded to 37.6. This is a high GL per serving due to the large rice portion.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Beef Gomtang Gukbap due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,860 mg per 600 g serving), which represents over 80% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-salt broth and avoiding added salt at the table is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from large white rice portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with Beef Gomtang Gukbap because the large white rice component produces a high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL approximately 37–38), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion by half, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, can substantially lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful that Beef Gomtang Gukbap contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g, approximately 5.4 g per serving) from beef shank and bone marrow, combined with high sodium from the broth; choosing lean beef cuts, skimming surface fat from the broth, and requesting low-sodium preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Gomtang Gukbap (소곰탕국밥) is a traditional Korean beef bone broth soup served with steamed white rice submerged directly into the bowl, a preparation style known as Gukbap. Gomtang is made by simmering beef bones — typically ox leg bones, knuckle bones, and beef shank — for many hours (often 6–12 hours) at a rolling boil, which extracts collagen, gelatin, and minerals from the bones and produces a characteristically milky-white, rich, and mildly flavoured broth. The dish is seasoned simply with salt and garnished with sliced spring onions, and may include accompaniments such as sweet Korean radish (mu), sliced tteok (rice cakes), a soft-boiled or poached egg, and a drizzle of sesame oil. The texture of the beef shank is tender and slightly gelatinous from the long braising. The broth is savoury, clean, and deeply umami without being heavily spiced. Gomtang Gukbap is a comfort food staple in South Korea, traditionally eaten for breakfast or as a restorative meal. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from beef shank and egg, moderate carbohydrate from white rice and tteok, and is relatively low in fat per 100 g, though the large serving size means total energy, sodium, and glycemic load per meal are significant. The high sodium content from the bone broth is a concern for individuals with hypertension, and the large rice portion produces a high glycemic load relevant to Type 2 Diabetes management. In Singapore, this dish is served at Korean restaurants such as Ajumma's and represents an authentic representation of Korean home-style cooking.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source entry exists for Beef Gomtang Gukbap as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated using a weighted-component method from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients (beef shank, white rice, beef broth, egg, sesame oil, radish), cross-referenced against Korean RDA food composition data for Gomtang. GI is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component using University of Sydney GI Database values. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; no single approved-source entry for the composite Gomtang Gukbap dish exists. Values should be updated if a direct HPB Singapore or Korean RDA entry becomes available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is set to 0.0; beef may contain trace natural trans fats (vaccenic acid) but these are below the threshold for reporting in the component sources and are confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data. 5. Serving size of 600 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl including broth, rice, beef, and accompaniments; actual restaurant portion may vary. 6. Sodium content is a significant concern; the estimate of 310 mg/100 g (approximately 1,860 mg per serving) is based on typical Korean bone broth sodium levels from USDA beef broth data and is consistent with published Korean dietary survey data. 7. The tteok (rice cake) component described in the input as 'sliced Toppoki' refers to sliced cylindrical tteok used in soups, not the spicy tteokbokki preparation. 8. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant source (Ajumma's) is not confirmed halal-certified. 9. GI is an analogous estimate; glycemic_index set to 54 based on white rice GI diluted by non-glycemic components. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 or less is classified as 'low'. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
